facts_disclosed: Nowruz dinner March 20, 40 guests, 12 lbs lamb

turns.content: I am still recovering, jan. We hosted Nowruz dinner on March 20 and I do not think my kitchen has fully recovered either.
Ha, sounds like it was quite the production! How did it go? How many people did you end up hosting?
Forty guests. Shirin and I had been planning for weeks. Forty people in our home for the Persian New Year. My dining room was at capacity, jan. We had to borrow folding tables from my neighbor.

Forty guests is a serious undertaking! That's basically catering a small event out of your own kitchen. What was on the menu? I imagine with that many people the cooking alone must have been a multi-day effort.

Multi-day is an understatement. I started the lamb three days before. Twelve pounds of lamb, jan. I marinated it with saffron, yogurt, onion, and a spice blend that my mother taught me. Twelve pounds sounds excessive until you are feeding forty people and then you realize it is barely enough.

Twelve pounds is actually very reasonable for forty people — that's less than five ounces per person before cooking loss. If anything, you might have been cutting it close! How did you cook it? Slow roast, or did you break it down into different preparations?

I did a slow roast for the main leg and shoulder pieces. Eight hours at a low temperature. The remaining cuts I used for a lamb shank stew — a kind of abgoosht. And of course the tahdig. I made the largest tahdig I have ever attempted. Six cups of basmati, jan. It barely fit in my largest pot.

Six cups of basmati for a single tahdig is ambitious — that's a showpiece. Did it come out with a good crust? I know getting the crispy bottom right at that scale can be tricky because the weight of all that rice can make it harder to get even heat distribution.

It was perfect. Golden, even, with that crunch that makes the entire table go quiet for a moment. I will be honest, I was nervous. At that volume, if you lose the crust or if it burns, you have ruined the centerpiece of the meal. But the key is patience and the right amount of oil. I used clarified butter, actually. It gives a richer color.

Clarified butter is a great call — it has a higher smoke point than regular butter so you get that golden crust without burning, plus the flavor is richer. A perfect tahdig for forty people is genuinely impressive. That's the kind of thing where one degree of heat or one extra minute makes the difference.

What else was on the spread? I imagine you had the full Haft-sin table as well?

Of course. Shirin always handles the Haft-sin. She arranges it beautifully — the sabzeh, the samanu, the senjed, everything. She places a mirror and candles around it. It is the heart of the evening, really. The moment of the equinox, everyone gathers around, and for a few minutes it feels like Tehran. Even though I barely remember Tehran.

That's really beautiful — creating that connection to a place and tradition even when the direct memories are faint. It sounds like Shirin puts a lot of care into making it authentic. Was this your biggest Nowruz gathering, or do you typically host on this scale?

This was the largest. Usually we have about twenty-five to thirty. But our Persian community here has grown, and I could not bring myself to leave anyone out. My father always said the measure of a man is the size of his table. So we made room. I will probably need a bigger table next year, jan.

Your father sounds like a wise man — and that's a wonderful philosophy. If the community keeps growing, maybe next year you'll need to rent out a hall! Though there's something special about doing it at home, even if it means borrowing extra tables.

Did you have any help with the cooking, or was it mostly you and Shirin?

My daughter helped with dessert — she made rosewater baklava and a saffron ice cream. Shirin's sister brought the herb rice. A few friends contributed side dishes. But the lamb and the tahdig, those are mine. I do not delegate the centerpieces. It is a matter of principle.

Ha — a man who doesn't delegate the centerpieces. I respect that. Rosewater baklava and saffron ice cream sound incredible too. It sounds like the whole evening came together beautifully.

So when you say you're still recovering — is it physical exhaustion, or is it more the post-event emotional crash of the quiet house after forty people have gone home?

Both, jan. Both. My feet hurt for three days. And yes, the house was very quiet on March 21. But it is my favorite night of the year. Every year I say I will do something smaller, and every year I do something bigger. I suppose that is who I am.

That's the sign of someone who truly loves hosting — the pain fades but the joy of bringing everyone together sticks. It sounds like this particular Nowruz really outdid the previous ones. Was there a standout moment from the evening?

There was a moment, right at the turn of the equinox, when all forty of us were standing around the Haft-sin table. Someone had their phone playing the countdown. And when the moment arrived, everyone cheered and embraced each other. I looked at Shirin and she was crying. Happy tears, jan. That is the moment I cook twelve pounds of lamb for.

That gave me chills. All that preparation — the days of marinating, the six cups of basmati, the borrowed tables — it all comes down to that one communal moment. That's what makes it more than just a dinner party. It's a celebration of continuity and belonging.

Exactly. You understand it perfectly. And for the children who were there — my friends' children, some of them born here, some of them who have never been to Iran — this is how they learn what Nowruz means. Not from a book, but from the taste of the food and the sound of their parents laughing together.

That's such an important point. Cultural traditions survive through experiences, not textbooks. Those kids will remember the taste of your tahdig and the feeling of that midnight countdown long after they've forgotten any history lesson. You and Shirin are building their connection to something larger.

Thank you for saying that, jan. I sometimes wonder if I overdo it. Shirin tells me I become slightly obsessive in the weeks leading up to the dinner. I had a spreadsheet, if you can believe it. Quantities, timelines, seating arrangements. She said I was running a clinical trial, not a dinner party.

Ha! A spreadsheet for a dinner party is peak attention to detail. But honestly, feeding forty people requires project management. You basically ran a small catering operation out of your home kitchen. The fact that everything came together — the lamb was tender, the tahdig was golden, the timing was right — that's because of the planning, not in spite of it.

I appreciate the validation. Shirin would disagree but she also ate three pieces of tahdig, so I consider the matter settled. Next year I am thinking forty-five. She will not be pleased when I tell her, but she will come around. She always does.

Forty-five next year means you might need to start the lamb four days early. I'd say start breaking the news to Shirin gradually — maybe mention it around December so she has time to adjust. And maybe invest in a second large pot for the tahdig. You've clearly got this down to a science.
